preamble: The shift from [tʰ] to [ts] is known as assibilation, a process where a stop consonant develops into an affricate. This is a common stage in consonant shifts, notably the High German Consonant Shift.

examples.note: The High German Consonant Shift involved the assibilation of [t] (often aspirated [tʰ]) to [ts] (initially) or [s] (medially/finally). E.g., English 'two' vs. German 'zwei' [tsvaɪ].
Allophonic assibilation of /t/ to [ts] before the high back vowel /u/.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
